title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: Modern minimalism is little more than an aesthetic for the privileged—a way for those who already have everything to perform a curated version of poverty. The 'less is more' mantra often involves replacing many cheap, functional items with a few exorbitantly expensive, 'sustainably made' pieces. This is not a rejection of consumerism; it is its most refined and elitist form. True simplicity should be about reducing our mental clutter and social obligations, not about the color of our walls or the number of spoons in our kitchen drawer.
texts.B: The psychological benefits of a decluttered environment are supported by clear evidence. Our brains are not designed to process the constant visual and cognitive noise of the modern digital and physical landscape. By intentionally limiting our possessions, we regain the capacity for deep focus and genuine connection. While some may use minimalism as a status symbol, for the majority it is a necessary survival strategy in an age of overwhelm. It is a tool for mental health, not just a trend for interior designers.
texts.C: I share the skepticism regarding the 'minimalist aesthetic.' A white room with a single designer chair is not a revolution; it’s a photograph. However, I disagree that minimalism is inherently elitist. At its core, it is a powerful critique of the debt-fueled growth model of our economy. By choosing to want less, we reclaim our time from the 'work-to-spend' cycle. The focus should be on the 'intentionality' of our choices rather than the final count of our belongings. It is about reclaiming agency in a system that wants us to be passive consumers.
texts.D: Minimalism is a predictable reaction to the excess of the late 20th century, but it risks becoming another form of rigid perfectionism. The pressure to have a 'perfectly curated' life can be just as stressful as the mess it seeks to replace. I agree that reducing our physical footprint is ecologically responsible, but we must be careful not to moralize our possessions. Owning things does not make one a bad person, and 'getting rid of everything' can often be a wasteful process of discarding perfectly functional items only to buy 'minimalist' versions later.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on whether minimalism is essentially a form of elitist consumerism?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls it 'elitist' and a 'refined form of consumerism.' Expert C disagrees, arguing it is a 'powerful critique' of the economic model and a way to reclaim time.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's opinion about the mental health benefits of reducing clutter?
questions.explanation: Expert B highlights the 'psychological benefits' and 'mental health.' Expert D also notes that reducing the physical footprint is 'ecologically responsible' and agrees that excessive clutter creates 'mess' and stress.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to A regarding the superficiality of the minimalist 'look'?
questions.explanation: Expert A criticizes the focus on the 'color of our walls.' Expert C shares this skepticism, calling the 'white room with a designer chair' just a photograph and not a true revolution.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the emotional impact of striving for a minimalist lifestyle?
questions.explanation: Expert D warns that minimalism can become 'rigid perfectionism' and a source of stress. Expert B argues it is a 'necessary survival strategy' that helps regain focus and connection.
